content:
7 min read 10 hours ago -- A Signal does not update the DOM directly. It tells Angular which reactive consumers may need attention. That distinction explains almost everything important about Angular Signals. Signals are often described as Angular’s replacement for Zone.js or traditional change detection. That explanation is attractive — but incomplete. Signals do not remove Angular’s rendering system. They give Angular something it previously had much less of: an explicit relationship between state and the code that consumes it. 1. The Problem: Angular Needs to Know Something Changed Imagine a dashboard containing: • A live stock-price ticker • A user-settings panel • A notification counter • A shopping-cart summary • Several unrelated widgets A price update affects only the ticker and perhaps a few calculations derived from it. Historically, Zone.js helped Angular notice that an asynchronous event had occurred — such as a timer completing, an HTTP response arriving, or a user event firing. But Zone.js could not explain the business meaning of that event. It could tell Angular: “Something happened.” It could not tell Angular: “The price state changed, and these are the consumers that depend on it.” Angular would therefore run change detection and evaluate the relevant component views to determine what had changed. OnPush could significantly reduce this work by allowing Angular to skip unaffected component subtrees. Signals add a more explicit notification mechanism. When a Signal changes, Angular already knows which reactive consumers previously read it. 2. What Is a Signal Mechanically? A Signal is a wrapper around a value. readonly count = signal(0);You read its value by calling it: this.count();The parentheses are important. Calling the Signal does two things: • It returns the current value. • When called inside a reactive context, it records that the current consumer depends on that Signal. Reactive consumers include: • computed() • effect() • Component templates • Other reactive Angular APIs This creates a relationship between a producer and a consumer. • The Signal is the producer. • The template, computed value, or effect is the consumer. Angular’s documentation describes Signals as a system that granularly tracks how and where state is used. 3. How Dependency Tracking Works Consider this example: readonly count = signal(0);readonly doubled = computed(() => { return this.count() * 2; });The first time doubled() is read, Angular executes its computation. While that computation is running, it reads count(). Angular records the relationship: count → doubledLater, when count changes: this.count.set(5);Angular knows that the cached value of doubled is no longer valid. But doubled does not necessarily recalculate immediately. It is marked stale and recalculated the next time someone reads it. That makes computed Signals both: • Lazy — they calculate only when read. • Memoised — they reuse the cached value until a dependency changes. console.log(this.doubled()); // Calculates: 10 console.log(this.doubled()); // Returns cached value: 10Computed dependencies are also dynamic. Angular tracks only the Signals that were actually read during the latest computation. 4. Visualising the Difference Event-driven change detection Async event occurs ↓ Angular is notified ↓ Change detection is scheduled ↓ Relevant component views are checked ↓ Changed DOM values are updatedWith the eager/default strategy, Angular may check a large part of the application tree. With OnPush, Angular can skip subtrees that have not been marked for checking. Signal-driven notification Signal changes ↓ Dependent consumers are invalidated ↓ Angular schedules the required rendering work ↓ Relevant views are refreshedSignals do not mean that Angular bypasses change detection and mutates one binding directly. The important improvement is that Angular receives an explicit notification from the state primitive and knows which consumer views depend on it. In zoneless applications, updating a Signal that was read in a template is one of the core notifications Angular uses to schedule change detection. 5. Reading Signals in Templates Consider an OnPush component: @Component({ selector: 'app-price-ticker', changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush, template: `

Current price: {{ price() }}

`, }) export class PriceTickerComponent { readonly price = signal(100); }When Angular renders the template, it observes that the component read price(). The component becomes a consumer of that Signal. When the price changes: this.price.set(105);Angular marks the component so that it will be refreshed during the next scheduled change-detection run. The dependency is tracked at the component-view level — not as a magical direct pointer that updates one DOM text node without Angular rendering the view. 6. Real Example: A Trading Dashboard Suppose a page contains: Dashboard ├── Price ticker ├── Market chart ├── User settings ├── Account profile └── Notification panelThe price changes several times per second. With broadly triggered, eager change detection, Angular may evaluate considerably more of the page than the price update logically requires. Signals improve the model because: • The ticker template explicitly reads the price Signal. • Price-related computed values depend on that Signal. • Unrelated computed values are not invalidated. • Signal-compatible views can be marked precisely. • OnPush or zoneless scheduling can help Angular avoid unnecessary subtree work. That does not guarantee a dramatic performance improvement in every application. For a small component with infrequent updates, the difference may be unnoticeable. Signals matter most when the application contains: • Frequently changing state • Expensive derived calculations • Large component trees • Several independent pieces of state • Real-time interfaces And performance claims should still be verified using Angular DevTools rather than assumed. 7. Derive State with computed() Suppose a cart contains several products: interface CartItem { price: number; quantity: number; }readonly cart = signal([]);readonly total = computed(() => this.cart().reduce( (sum, item) => sum + item.price * item.quantity, 0 ) );You do not need to manually synchronise another total Signal whenever the cart changes. The dependency is declared through the computation itself: cart → totalWhenever cart changes, total becomes stale. The next time total() is read, Angular recalculates it and caches the result. This prevents an entire category of bugs in which derived state becomes inconsistent with its source. 8. Plain Getter vs computed() A common explanation says that wrapping a Signal read inside a plain getter breaks dependency tracking. That is not accurate. This still reads the underlying Signal: get total(): number { return this.cart().reduce( (sum, item) => sum + item.price * item.quantity, 0 ); }If Angular evaluates this getter while rendering a template, the cart() read still occurs inside the template’s reactive context. The Signal can therefore still be tracked. The real problem is different: A plain getter is not memoised. Angular may execute the getter every time that component view is checked — even when cart has not changed. A computed() value: • Caches its result • Recalculates only after a dependency changes • Clearly communicates that the value is derived state • Can be reused by several consumers Therefore, this is usually preferable: readonly total = computed(() => this.cart().reduce( (sum, item) => sum + item.price * item.quantity, 0 ) );The advantage is memoisation and reactive modelling — not the restoration of otherwise-lost Signal tracking. 9. Use effect() Carefully An effect runs once initially and tracks the Signals read during that execution. effect(() => { console.log(`Price changed to ${this.price()}`); });When a tracked Signal changes, the effect runs again. Effects are useful when Signal state must be synchronised with a non-reactive API, such as: • Browser storage • Analytics • Logging • Canvas rendering • Third-party charting libraries They should not normally be used to copy state from one Signal into another. Use computed() for derived state. Use effect() for actual side effects. Angular’s current guidance describes effects as an API that should be reached for only when synchronising with imperative, non-reactive systems. Effect cleanup An effect can register cleanup logic: effect((onCleanup) => { const currentPrice = this.price(); const timer = window.setTimeout(() => { this.showPriceUpdatedMessage(currentPrice); }, 500); onCleanup(() => { window.clearTimeout(timer); }); });The cleanup callback runs: • Before the effect executes again • When the effect is destroyed This prevents previous timers, subscriptions, or other resources from leaking across executions. 10. Signals Do Not Replace RxJS Signals and RxJS overlap in some situations, but they are designed around different problems. Signals are excellent for: • Local UI state • Synchronous state • Derived values • Template state • Fine-grained dependency tracking RxJS is excellent for: • Debouncing • Cancelling requests • Retrying failed operations • Combining asynchronous sources • Time-based streams • Event pipelines For example: readonly searchQuery = signal('');A Signal is ideal for storing the current search query. But RxJS remains useful for handling: query changes → debounce for 300 ms → cancel previous request → call search API → retry or handle errorsThe practical Angular architecture is rarely: Signals or RxJS? It is usually: Signals for state and derivation; RxJS for asynchronous stream composition. Common Mistakes 1. Forgetting the parentheses this.count // Signal object this.count() // Signal value2. Mutating objects without updating the Signal this.user().name = 'Pranav';Prefer producing a new value: this.user.update(user => ({ ...user, name: 'Pranav', }));3. Using effect() to propagate derived state effect(() => { this.total.set(this.calculateTotal(this.cart())); });Prefer: readonly total = computed(() => this.calculateTotal(this.cart()) );4. Assuming every update becomes faster automatically Signals improve notification and dependency tracking. They do not fix: • Expensive template expressions • Poor component boundaries • Large DOM structures • Unnecessary object creation • Bad list-rendering strategies 5. Assuming Signals eliminate change detection Signals participate in Angular’s rendering and change-detection system. They make state changes explicit and help Angular schedule more targeted work. Interview Question What happens when a dependency of a computed Signal changes? A strong answer: The computed Signal is invalidated, but its derivation does not necessarily execute immediately. Computed Signals are lazy and memoised. The derivation runs again the next time the computed value is read, and the result is then cached until another dependency changes. Why does that matter? Because a derived value that nobody reads does not need to consume CPU recalculating after every upstream update. Key Takeaways • Signals create producer-to-consumer relationships between state and reactive consumers. • Reading a Signal inside a reactive context registers the dependency. • Templates track Signals at the component-view level. • computed() values are lazy and memoised. • Plain getters do not automatically break tracking, but they do not provide computed memoisation. • effect() is intended for synchronising Signal state with imperative APIs. • Signals improve change notification; they do not bypass Angular’s rendering system. • Signals and RxJS solve different — but complementary — problems. One important modern-Angular detail: zoneless change detection is enabled by default in Angular 21+, making explicit Angular notifications — including template-read Signal updates — even more central to how rendering is scheduled.
